quiz.title: Short-Run Phillips Curve
quiz.subtitle: Unit 10 Quiz 2: Trade-offs, Shifts, Expectations, and Policy
quiz.subject: Macroeconomics
quiz.topic: First Year - SRPC Analysis, Expectations, Supply Shocks, Stabilization Policy

questions.question: Along the short-run Phillips curve, a reduction in the unemployment rate is achieved by:
questions.explanation: Higher aggregate demand moves the economy up along the SRPC to lower unemployment and higher inflation.

questions.question: If the natural rate of unemployment is 5% and actual unemployment is 4%, according to the SRPC:
options: Actual inflation is below expected inflation
Actual inflation is above expected inflation
questions.explanation: When unemployment is below the natural rate, actual inflation exceeds expected inflation in the SRPC framework.

questions.question: A negative supply shock that raises production costs will cause:
options: A movement down along the SRPC
A movement up along the SRPC
A rightward shift of the SRPC
A leftward shift of the SRPC
questions.explanation: Negative supply shocks (e.g., higher oil prices) increase inflation at every unemployment rate, shifting the SRPC right.

questions.question: When workers and firms adjust their inflation expectations upward, the short-run Phillips curve:
questions.explanation: Higher expected inflation shifts the SRPC right (or up), meaning more inflation at each unemployment rate.

questions.question: Which of the following best explains why the SRPC is downward sloping?
options: Nominal wages are slow to adjust to price changes
The velocity of money is constant
The economy is always at full employment
questions.explanation: Sticky nominal wages mean higher prices reduce real wages, leading firms to hire more and lower unemployment.

questions.question: A decrease in expected inflation will shift the SRPC:
questions.explanation: Lower expected inflation reduces inflation at every unemployment rate, shifting the SRPC leftward (or downward).

questions.question: If the central bank announces a credible policy to reduce inflation, and the public lowers its inflation expectations accordingly, the SRPC will:
questions.explanation: Credible disinflation lowers expected inflation, shifting the SRPC left and allowing lower inflation with less unemployment cost.

questions.question: Stagflation is characterized by:
questions.explanation: Stagflation combines high inflation with high unemployment, typically caused by a rightward shift of the SRPC (negative supply shock).

questions.question: Which of the following would cause a movement along the SRPC (not a shift)?
options: A change in the natural rate of unemployment
questions.explanation: Changes in government spending affect aggregate demand, moving the economy along the existing SRPC.

questions.question: According to the expectations-augmented Phillips curve, if actual inflation equals expected inflation, then unemployment is:
questions.explanation: When inflation turns out exactly as people expected, workers and firms have already built that inflation into wage and price decisions. With no surprises, the economy operates at its natural level of unemployment.

questions.question: A permanent decrease in the natural rate of unemployment will cause the SRPC to:
questions.explanation: A lower natural rate means the long-run Phillips curve shifts left, and the SRPC shifts left with it (lower unemployment at each inflation rate).

questions.question: If adaptive expectations hold, a sustained expansionary policy will lead to:
options: A permanently higher inflation rate with unemployment returning to natural rate
questions.explanation: With adaptive expectations, unemployment returns to the natural rate, but inflation remains higher (accelerationist hypothesis).

questions.question: Which of the following shifts the SRPC rightward?
options: A rise in global commodity prices
A decrease in the money supply
questions.explanation: Higher commodity prices (e.g., oil) are a negative supply shock, raising inflation at every unemployment rate — shifting SRPC right.

questions.question: The slope of the SRPC depends primarily on:
options: The degree of wage and price stickiness
questions.explanation: More sticky wages → flatter SRPC (large unemployment change for small inflation change). Less sticky → steeper SRPC.

questions.question: If the public has rational expectations, an anticipated increase in the money supply will:
questions.explanation: With rational expectations, anticipated policy is fully priced into wages and prices, so real variables like unemployment do not change.

questions.question: A favorable supply shock (e.g., falling energy prices) will cause the SRPC to shift:
questions.explanation: Lower production costs reduce inflation at every unemployment rate, shifting the SRPC leftward (and downward).

questions.question: When the economy moves from point A to point B along a stable SRPC, which of the following must have occurred?
options: A change in the natural rate
questions.explanation: Movement along the SRPC is caused by changes in aggregate demand (monetary or fiscal policy).

questions.question: The short-run trade-off between inflation and unemployment exists because:
options: Wages and prices are fully flexible
questions.explanation: Nominal rigidities (sticky wages/prices) allow demand changes to affect real output and unemployment in the short run.

questions.question: If the natural rate of unemployment rises from 5% to 6%, the SRPC will:
questions.explanation: A higher natural rate shifts the long-run Phillips curve right; the SRPC also shifts right because it is anchored to the LRPC.

questions.question: According to the SRPC model, a central bank that wants to reduce inflation quickly will likely cause:
questions.explanation: Contractionary policy moves down along the SRPC, temporarily raising unemployment while lowering inflation (disinflationary recession).

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You have a strong command of SRPC shifts, movements, expectations, and supply shocks.
scoreMessages.70: Good work. Review the difference between AD-driven movements and expectation/supply shock shifts.
scoreMessages.50: Solid effort. Focus on what shifts the curve (expectations, supply shocks, natural rate) vs. what moves along it (AD changes).
scoreMessages.0: Keep studying. Remember: SRPC = short-run trade-off; shifts come from expected inflation, supply shocks, or natural rate changes.
